code: set.seed(42) m = 15; T_len = 8; p = 2  W = array(0, dim = c(m, m, p)) geo = matrix(runif(m * m), m, m); geo = (geo + t(geo)) / 2; diag(geo) = 0 W[,,1] = geo groups = sample(1:3, m, replace = TRUE) W[,,2] = outer(groups, groups, "==") * 1.0; diag(W[,,2]) = 0 dimnames(W) = list(paste0("n", 1:m), paste0("n", 1:m), c("proximity", "shared_group"))  Z = array(0, dim = c(m, m, 1, T_len)) dist_mat = matrix(rnorm(m * m), m, m); dist_mat = (dist_mat + t(dist_mat)) / 2 diag(dist_mat) = NA for (t in 1:T_len) Z[,,1,t] = dist_mat dimnames(Z)[[3]] = "distance"  alpha_true = c(1, 0.3); beta_true = c(0.5, 0.2); theta_true = -0.1 A_true = alpha_true[1] * W[,,1] + alpha_true[2] * W[,,2] B_true = beta_true[1] * W[,,1] + beta_true[2] * W[,,2]  Y = array(NA, dim = c(m, m, T_len)) Y[,,1] = matrix(rpois(m * m, 2), m, m); diag(Y[,,1]) = NA for (t in 2:T_len) {     X_t = log(Y[,,t-1] + 1); X_t[is.na(X_t)] = 0     eta = theta_true * Z[,,1,t] + A_true %*% X_t %*% t(B_true)     lambda = pmin(exp(eta), 100)     Y[,,t] = matrix(rpois(m * m, c(lambda)), m, m); diag(Y[,,t]) = NA }  X = array(0, dim = c(m, m, T_len)) for (t in 2:T_len) X[,,t] = log(Y[,,t-1] + 1) X[is.na(X)] = 0 fit_als = sir(     Y = Y, W = W, X = X, Z = Z,     family = "poisson", method = "ALS", calc_se = TRUE )

title: Getting started with sir
text: Standard regression approaches relational data treat dyad independent observation, ignoring higher-order dependencies generated actors respond one another’s past behavior. sir package fits Social Influence Regression (SIR) models account dependencies regressing current network outcomes lagged network state, decomposing influence sender receiver channels parameterized observable covariates. model specifies expected outcome directed edge (,j)(,j) time tt : μi,j,t=𝛉⊤𝐳i,j,t+∑k,ℓxk,ℓ,tai,kbj,ℓ\mu_{,j,t} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^\top \mathbf{z}_{,j,t} + \sum_{k,\ell} x_{k,\ell,t} \, a_{,k} \, b_{j,\ell} 𝐳i,j,t\mathbf{z}_{,j,t} exogenous covariates coefficients 𝛉\boldsymbol{\theta}, xk,ℓ,tx_{k,\ell,t} lagged network state (typically log(yk,ℓ,t−1+1)\log(y_{k,\ell,t-1} + 1)), ai,ka_{,k} captures sender influence (predictive node kk’s past sending node ii’s current sending), bj,ℓb_{j,\ell} captures receiver influence (past targeting node ℓ\ell predicts current targeting node jj). influence matrices 𝐀\mathbf{} 𝐁\mathbf{B} parameterized influence covariates 𝐖\mathbf{W}: 𝐀=∑r=1pαr𝐖r𝐁=∑r=1pβr𝐖r\mathbf{} = \sum_{r=1}^{p} \alpha_r \mathbf{W}_r \qquad \mathbf{B} = \sum_{r=1}^{p} \beta_r \mathbf{W}_r α1=1\alpha_1 = 1 fixed identifiability. See Methodology article full mathematical framework.

title: Getting started with sir
text: illustrate workflow, simulate small directed network Poisson counts, exogenous distance covariate, two influence covariates (geographic proximity shared group membership). data-generating process follows SIR specification directly, know true parameter values can evaluate model’s ability recover . produces network 15 nodes 8 periods. Edge counts Poisson, driven distance covariate (θ=−0.1\theta = -0.1) network influence flowing geographic proximity shared group membership.
code: set.seed(42)  m = 15     # number of nodes T_len = 8  # number of time periods p = 2      # number of influence covariates  # influence covariates W = array(0, dim = c(m, m, p))  # W1: geographic proximity (symmetric, decays with distance) geo = matrix(runif(m * m), m, m) geo = (geo + t(geo)) / 2 diag(geo) = 0 W[,,1] = geo  # W2: shared group membership (block structure) groups = sample(1:3, m, replace = TRUE) shared = outer(groups, groups, "==") * 1.0 diag(shared) = 0 W[,,2] = shared  dimnames(W) = list(     paste0("n", 1:m), paste0("n", 1:m),     c("proximity", "shared_group") )  # true influence parameters alpha_true = c(1, 0.3) beta_true  = c(0.5, 0.2)  # build true influence matrices A_true = matrix(0, m, m) for (r in 1:p) A_true = A_true + alpha_true[r] * W[,,r] B_true = matrix(0, m, m) for (r in 1:p) B_true = B_true + beta_true[r] * W[,,r]  # exogenous covariate: dyadic distance (time-invariant) Z = array(0, dim = c(m, m, 1, T_len)) distance = matrix(rnorm(m * m, mean = 0, sd = 1), m, m) distance = (distance + t(distance)) / 2 diag(distance) = NA for (t in 1:T_len) Z[,,1,t] = distance dimnames(Z)[[3]] = "distance"  theta_true = -0.1  # simulate Y as Poisson counts Y = array(NA, dim = c(m, m, T_len)) dimnames(Y) = list(paste0("n", 1:m), paste0("n", 1:m), paste0("t", 1:T_len))  # first period: baseline rates Y[,,1] = matrix(rpois(m * m, lambda = 2), m, m) diag(Y[,,1]) = NA  # subsequent periods: influenced by lagged network for (t in 2:T_len) {     X_t = log(Y[,,t-1] + 1)     X_t[is.na(X_t)] = 0     eta = theta_true * Z[,,1,t] + A_true %*% X_t %*% t(B_true)     lambda = exp(eta)     lambda[lambda > 100] = 100     Y[,,t] = matrix(rpois(m * m, lambda = c(lambda)), m, m)     diag(Y[,,t]) = NA }  # construct X as log-transformed lagged Y X = array(0, dim = c(m, m, T_len)) for (t in 2:T_len) {     X[,,t] = log(Y[,,t-1] + 1) } X[is.na(X)] = 0

title: Social Influence Regression Models
text: Relational outcomes political social systems rarely independent. country ii initiates conflict country kk time tt, action may reshape likelihood country jj time t+1t+1, particularly ii jj share alliance, geographic proximity, common adversary. Standard regression approaches network data treat directed dyad independent observation, conditioning covariates discarding higher-order dependencies constitute network influence. Social Influence Regression (SIR) model addresses limitation directly. Rather treating network influence nuisance latent quantity absorbed random effects, model estimates influence function observable covariates. framework operates longitudinal network data (time series n×nn \times n relational matrices) asks past interactions across network predict current outcomes, dyad-level actor-level covariates account predictive patterns. methodological framework introduced Minhas Hoff (2025), “Decomposing Network Dynamics: Social Influence Regression,” Political Analysis.

title: Social Influence Regression Models
text: SIR model specifies expected outcome directed edge (,j)(,j) time tt : μi,j,t=𝐳i,j,tT𝛉+∑k,ℓxk,ℓ,tai,kbj,ℓ\mu_{,j,t} = \mathbf{z}_{,j,t}^T \boldsymbol{\theta} + \sum_{k,\ell} x_{k,\ell,t} a_{,k} b_{j,\ell} first term standard regression outcome exogenous covariates 𝐳i,j,t\mathbf{z}_{,j,t} coefficients 𝛉\boldsymbol{\theta}. second term captures network influence: ai,ka_{,k} measures predictive node kk’s past sending behavior node ii’s current sending, bj,ℓb_{j,\ell} captures analogous relationship receiver side, network state xk,ℓ,tx_{k,\ell,t} (typically lagged outcomes) carries influence signal matrices. influence matrices 𝐀\mathbf{} 𝐁\mathbf{B} parameterized known influence covariates 𝐖r\mathbf{W}_r (geographic distance, alliance ties, shared attributes): 𝐀=∑r=1pαr𝐖r,α1=1 (fixed identifiability)\mathbf{} = \sum_{r=1}^{p} \alpha_r \mathbf{W}_r, \quad \alpha_1 = 1 \text{ (fixed identifiability)}𝐁=∑r=1pβr𝐖r\mathbf{B} = \sum_{r=1}^{p} \beta_r \mathbf{W}_r parameterization reduces parameter count O(n2)O(n^2) O(p)O(p), pp number influence covariates. importantly, makes estimated influence patterns directly interpretable: α\alpha β\beta coefficients identify covariates matter influence much.

title: Social Influence Regression Models
text: package provides two estimation approaches. default, Alternating Least Squares (ALS), exploits bilinear structure model iterating GLM sub-problems (𝛉,𝛂)(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}) (𝛉,𝛃)(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{\beta}). sub-problem standard generalized linear model, full estimation reduces sequence low-dimensional optimizations. generally stable high-dimensional problems substantially faster Bayesian approach originally used bilinear network autoregressions. direct BFGS optimizer (method = "optim") also available uses analytical gradients computed via C++; can converge faster small networks tends less stable number influence covariates large. Standard errors computed Hessian (classical) sandwich estimator (robust). Hessian ill-conditioned, common bilinear models, boot_sir() provides bootstrap standard errors via block resampling time periods parametric simulation.

title: Social Influence Regression Models
text: model supports Poisson (count outcomes log link), Normal (continuous outcomes identity link), Binomial (binary outcomes logit link) families.

title: Bootstrap Inference for SIR Model Parameters — boot_sir
text: object class "boot_sir" components: coefs R x n_params matrix bootstrap coefficient estimates.     Rows failed replicates contain NA. se Named numeric vector bootstrap standard errors (one per     parameter). ci_lo Lower 2.5% percentile bounds. ci_hi Upper 97.5% percentile bounds. point_est Point estimates original fit. param_names Character vector parameter names. n_valid Number successful bootstrap replicates. n_total Total number replicates attempted. type bootstrap type used. family distribution family.

title: Bootstrap Inference for SIR Model Parameters — boot_sir
text: Two bootstrap strategies available: block Resamples time periods replacement. Preserves     within-period dependence structure. Best T moderately large     (T >= 10). parametric Simulates new outcome arrays fitted model     using estimated parameters specified family distribution.     Better T small model well-specified. replicate refits full SIR model. Replicates fail converge dropped reported. Standard errors column standard deviations successful replicates. Confidence intervals use percentile method.

title: Calculate Linear Predictor (eta) for SIR Model — eta_tab

W Three-dimensional array (m x m x p) influence covariates. slice W[,,r] parameterizes influence structure. NULL p=0, network influence included. X Three-dimensional array (m x m x T) carrying network influence. Typically lagged outcomes. Required even W NULL (can zeros). Z Array exogenous covariates. Can : NULL q=0: exogenous effects 3D array (m x m x T): Single covariate 4D array (m x m x q x T): Multiple covariates fix_receiver Logical. TRUE, B fixed identity matrix tab parsed [theta, alpha_1:p] alpha free. Default FALSE.

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: Fits Social Influence Regression model network data social influence effects. SIR model captures network connections influence outcomes bilinear interaction terms, allowing sender receiver effects directed networks. model decomposes network influence two components: Sender influence (matrix): [,k] measures much node k's     behavior (via X) shapes node 's outgoing ties. Receiver influence (B matrix): B[j,l] measures node l's     position shapes node j's incoming ties.

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
code: sir(   Y,   W = NULL,   X = NULL,   Z = NULL,   family,   method = "ALS",   calc_se = TRUE,   fix_receiver = FALSE,   symmetric = FALSE,   bipartite = NULL,   kron_mode = FALSE,   ... )

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: Y three-dimensional array dimensions (m x m x T) containing network outcomes. Y[,j,t] represents directed outcome node node j time t. Can contain NA values missing observations. diagonal (self-loops) can included excluded depending application. W Optional influence covariate array, either: 3D array (m x m x p): Static influence covariates. W[,j,r]     represents r-th covariate edge j. W used     time periods. 4D array (m x m x p x T): Dynamic (time-varying) influence     covariates. W[,j,r,t] allows influence structure change time.     Parameters (alpha, beta) still estimated jointly across periods,     influence matrices A_t B_t vary t. ALS method     supported 4D W. Common choices include graph Laplacians, geographic distance matrices, node-level covariates expanded edge-level. NULL p=0, model uses identity matrices (network influence structure). X Optional three-dimensional array dimensions (m x m x T) representing network state carries influence. Typically lagged version Y (e.g., X[,,t] = Y[,,t-1]). NULL W provided, error thrown. X determines network patterns influence future outcomes. Z Optional array exogenous covariates. Can either: 3D array (m x m x T): Single covariate varying across edges time 4D array (m x m x q x T): Multiple (q) covariates Examples include dyadic covariates (trade agreements, geographic distance) node-level attributes (GDP, population) expanded edge-level. family Character string specifying distribution family link function. Must one "poisson", "normal", "binomial". choice depends nature outcome variable. method Character string specifying estimation method. Either "ALS" (Alternating Least Squares) "optim" (direct optimization via BFGS). Default "ALS" generally stable. calc_se Logical indicating whether calculate standard errors parameters. Standard errors computed using observed information matrix. Setting FALSE speeds computation uncertainty quantification needed. fix_receiver Logical. TRUE, fixes B = (identity matrix) estimates (theta, alpha). eliminates bilinear identification problem (scaling ambiguity B) removing receiver influence channel. model becomes standard GLM, yielding proper standard errors. Appropriate receiver effects negligible. Default FALSE. symmetric Logical. TRUE, treats network undirected (symmetric). function symmetrizes Y averaging upper lower triangles, uses upper-triangle observations fitting, sets fix_receiver = TRUE (since sender/receiver distinction meaningless undirected networks). Default FALSE. bipartite Logical NULL. Indicates whether network bipartite (senders receivers distinct node sets). NULL (default), bipartite status inferred Y: non-square arrays (n1 != n2) treated bipartite. Set TRUE explicitly square arrays senders receivers nonetheless distinct populations. Setting FALSE non-square Y raises error. Bipartite networks require fix_receiver = TRUE. kron_mode Logical. TRUE, replaces separate (alpha, beta) single p x p coefficient matrix C, C[r,s] weight W_r X W_s'. general fix bilinear identification problem. yet implemented. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments passed fitting functions: trace: Logical integer controlling output verbosity. tol: Convergence tolerance ALS (default 1e-8). max_iter: Maximum ALS iterations (default 100).

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: object class "sir" following components: summ Data frame parameter estimates columns coef,       se (classical SE), rse (robust/sandwich SE),       t_se (z-statistic using classical SE),       t_rse (z-statistic using robust SE). Row names identify       parameter. title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: SIR model specifies expected outcome directed edge node node j time t : $$\mu_{,j,t} = \theta^T z_{,j,t} + \sum_{k,l} X_{k,l,t} A_{,k} B_{j,l}$$ : \(\mu_{,j,t}\) expected value outcome Y_ijt \(\theta\) q-dimensional vector coefficients exogenous covariates \(z_{,j,t}\) q-dimensional vector exogenous covariates \(X_{k,l,t}\) represents network state (often lagged Y) carries influence \(A_{,k}\) represents node influenced behavior node k \(B_{j,l}\) represents node j's reception affected node l's position bilinear term \(\sum_{k,l} X_{k,l,t} A_{,k} B_{j,l}\) captures network influence can parameterized using influence covariates W : \(= \sum_{r=1}^{p} \alpha_r W_r\) (sender effects, \(\alpha_1 = 1\) fixed) \(B = \sum_{r=1}^{p} \beta_r W_r\) (receiver effects) parameterization reduces number parameters \(O(m^2)\) \(O(p)\), \(p \ll m\).

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: Alternating Least Squares (ALS): Iteratively optimizes given B, B given Generally stable high-dimensional problems Better sparse networks p large May converge local optima Direct Optimization (optim): Uses BFGS optimize parameters simultaneously Can faster small problems May provide better solutions good starting values available prone numerical issues high dimensions

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: Poisson: count data (e.g., number interactions) Link function: log Variance function: \(V(\mu) = \mu\) Use : Y_ijt represents counts Normal: continuous data (e.g., trade volumes, distances) Link function: identity Variance function: \(V(\mu) = \sigma^2\) Use : Y_ijt continuous approximately normal Binomial: binary data (e.g., presence/absence ties) Link function: logit Variance function: \(V(\mu) = \mu(1 - \mu)\) Use : Y_ijt binary (0/1)

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
text: Minhas, S. & Hoff, P. D. (2025). Social Influence Regression. Political Analysis.

title: Social Influence Regression (SIR) Model — sir
code: # \donttest{ set.seed(123) m <- 8; T_len <- 5; p <- 2 Y <- array(rpois(m * m * T_len, lambda = 2), dim = c(m, m, T_len)) X <- array(0, dim = c(m, m, T_len)) X[,,2:T_len] <- Y[,,1:(T_len - 1)] W <- array(rnorm(m * m * p), dim = c(m, m, p)) model <- sir(Y = Y, W = W, X = X, family = "poisson",              method = "ALS", calc_se = FALSE, max_iter = 10) print(model) #>  #> Social Influence Regression Model #> 8 nodes, 5 time periods (directed) #> Config: poisson | ALS #> Status: converged | N = 320 | Log-Lik: -576.96 | AIC: 1159.9 #> Coefficients: #>             Estimate #> (alphaW) W2   3.9283 #> (betaW) W1    0.0010 #> (betaW) W2    0.0020 #> (SEs not computed) #> Use `summary()` for detailed results coef(model) #> [1] 3.928314350 0.001010846 0.001972906 # }

title: Fit SIR Model via Alternating Least Squares (ALS) — sir_alsfit
text: Y Three-dimensional array (m x m x T) network outcomes. Missing values (NA) automatically handled excluding likelihood. algorithm uses complete case analysis within GLM step. W Three-dimensional array (m x m x p) influence covariates used parameterize influence matrices B. slice W[,,r] represents one influence covariate. NULL p=0, identity matrices used (influence). X Three-dimensional array (m x m x T) representing network state carries influence, typically lagged outcomes. Must provided W non-NULL. determines network patterns affect future outcomes. Z Four-dimensional array (m x m x q x T) exogenous covariates, NULL. covariates directly affect outcomes interact network influence structure. Examples include dyadic attributes time trends. family Character string specifying GLM family: "poisson", "normal", "binomial". determines link function variance structure used GLM step. trace Logical integer controlling verbosity: FALSE/0: output TRUE/1: Progress bar convergence message 2: Detailed iteration information including deviance tol Numeric convergence tolerance. algorithm stops relative change deviance less value. Default 1e-8. Smaller values give accurate results require iterations. max_iter Integer maximum number ALS iterations. Default 100. iteration consists one -step one B-step. Increase difficult problems starting far optimum. fix_receiver Logical. TRUE, fixes B = (identity matrix) estimates (theta, alpha) via single GLM step. eliminates bilinear identification problem removing receiver influence channel. model reduces standard GLM well-conditioned standard errors. Default FALSE. kron_mode Logical. TRUE, replaces separate (alpha, beta) single p x p coefficient matrix C. yet implemented. Default FALSE. dynamic_W Logical. TRUE, W treated 4D array (m x m x p x T) time-varying influence covariates. Design matrices constructed R rather C++. Default FALSE.

title: Fit SIR Model via Alternating Least Squares (ALS) — sir_alsfit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Example with simulated network data m <- 15 T <- 20 p <- 2  # Generate network with influence Y <- array(rpois(m*m*T, 3), dim=c(m,m,T)) X <- array(0, dim=c(m,m,T)) X[,,2:T] <- Y[,,1:(T-1)]  # Lagged Y  # Influence covariates (e.g., distance-based) W <- array(rnorm(m*m*p), dim=c(m,m,p))  # Fit using ALS fit <- sir_alsfit(Y, W, X, Z=NULL, family="poisson",                    trace=TRUE, tol=1e-6, max_iter=50)  # Examine convergence plot(fit$DEV[,2], type="l", ylab="Deviance", xlab="Iteration")  # Extract influence matrices A_matrix <- fit$A B_matrix <- fit$B } # }

title: Fit SIR Model via Direct Optimization — sir_optfit
text: Y Three-dimensional array (m x m x T) network outcomes. Missing values (NA) handled excluding likelihood. Large networks (m > 100) may cause memory issues. W Three-dimensional array (m x m x p) influence covariates. slice parameterizes influence matrices. NULL, network influence structure included (reduces standard GLM). X Three-dimensional array (m x m x T) representing network state carrying influence. Typically lagged Y. Required W provided. Z Four-dimensional array (m x m x q x T) exogenous covariates. enter model linearly without network interactions. family Character string: "poisson", "normal", "binomial". Determines likelihood link function used. trace Integer controlling optimizer output: 0: output (default) 1: Final convergence report 2: Progress iteration 3+: Detailed debugging information start Optional numeric vector starting values [theta, alpha, beta]. Length must equal q + 2p. NULL, uses smart initialization. Good starting values dramatically improve convergence.

title: Fit SIR Model via Direct Optimization — sir_optfit
text: list class "sir_optim_fit" containing: tab Vector optimized parameters [theta, alpha, beta] m x m sender effects matrix B m x m receiver effects matrix convergence Convergence code optim (0 = success) message Convergence message optimizer iterations Number function evaluations value Final negative log-likelihood hessian Approximate Hessian optimum (requested) gradient Final gradient (near zero)

title: Fit SIR Model via Direct Optimization — sir_optfit
text: function treats SIR negative log-likelihood single optimization problem parameters (theta, alpha, beta). uses BFGS quasi-Newton method analytical gradients C++ backend. starting values provided, theta initialized fitting GLM ignoring network effects, alpha/beta set small random values. Convergence code 0 indicates success; code 1 means maximum number iterations reached. supports static (3D) W.

title: Fit SIR Model via Direct Optimization — sir_optfit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Small network example m <- 10 T <- 15 p <- 2  Y <- array(rpois(m*m*T, 2), dim=c(m,m,T)) X <- array(0, dim=c(m,m,T)) X[,,2:T] <- Y[,,1:(T-1)] W <- array(rnorm(m*m*p, sd=0.1), dim=c(m,m,p))  # Fit with direct optimization fit_optim <- sir_optfit(Y, W, X, Z=NULL,                         family="poisson", trace=1)                          # Check convergence if(fit_optim$convergence == 0) {   cat("Optimization successful\n")   print(fit_optim$tab) }  # Compare with ALS fit_als <- sir_alsfit(Y, W, X, Z=NULL,                       family="poisson")  # Often similar results but different paths } # }
